title: Penny Wise and Pound Foolish: Does Striving to Meet Earnings Expectations by Manipulating Real Activities Trigger Product Recalls?
abstract: We examine whether managers’ activities in striving to reach earnings targets through real earnings manipulation affect their firms’ product recalls. Our evidence implies that firms suspected of manipulating real activities in trying to meet earnings benchmarks exhibit a higher likelihood and frequency of product recalls. In cross-sectional results consistent with expectations, we find that the impact of exploiting real activities to attain earnings benchmarks on product recalls intensifies for firms whose managers have stronger incentives to manage earnings and subsides for firms subject to greater customer power and firms with more growth opportunities. Additional analysis shows that lowering product quality to meet or beat earnings expectations undermines firms’ future performance.

author.Yangyang Chen: Department of Accountancy, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong
author.Lijun Ma: Business School, University of International Business and Economics, Beijing 100029, China
author.Jeffrey Pittman: Faculty of Business Administration, Memorial University, St. John’s, Newfoundland and Labrador A1C 5S7, Canada; and Pamplin College of Business, Virginia Tech, Blacksburg, Virginia 24060
author.Xin Yang: Institute of Accounting and Finance, Shanghai University of Finance and Economics, Shanghai 200433, China

article.1. Introduction: Extensive prior research implies that managers have incentives to meet or beat the market’s earnings expectations by manipulating accruals or real activities at the expense of long-run firm prospects (e.g.,Bhojraj et al. 2009,Cohen and Zarowin 2010,Bereskin et al. 2018). Additionally,Graham et al. (2005)provide survey evidence that managers will likely forgo an investment project that enhances long-term value when it may lead to missing the consensus analyst earnings forecast. Besides shareholder value, recent research suggests that myopic actions stemming from fixating on meeting or beating earnings expectations have potential negative effects on the interests of other stakeholders, such as employees (Caskey and Ozel 2017) and the local community (Liu et al. 2021,Thomas et al. 2022). We extend prior work by examining whether the pressure to reach earnings benchmarks induces managers to manipulate firms’ real activities in the production process, which increases the likelihood and frequency of product recalls. Product recalls are the outcome of severe product quality failures. U.S. federal regulations require firms to suspend sales of the product and promptly report the issue to the relevant regulatory agencies after a safety defect becomes known. Product recalls are not only prevalent in the consumer market but also have serious adverse impacts on the recalling firms and their customers. From 2010 to 2015, U.S. federal agencies reported at least 4,000 product recalls each year. The recalled products are routinely associated with harmful effects on customers, such as injury or even death. Product recalls also have major negative implications for the recalling firms. Apart from the costs of replacing or repairing the defective products, product recalls can lead to various firm-level indirect costs, such as the loss of its goodwill, damage to its brand name reputation, the loss of sales, and even product liability lawsuits (Jarrell and Peltzman 1985,Hoffer et al. 1988,Barber and Darrough 1996), resulting in shareholders sustaining considerable wealth losses (Jarrell and Peltzman 1985,Pruitt and Peterson 1986,Lee et al. 2015,Kini et al. 2017). Given the prevalence of recalls and their adverse effects on customers, recalling firms, investors, and broader society along with the emergence of several high-profile product recall events in recent years (e.g., General Motors’s ignition switch recall in 2014, Volkswagen’s diesel engine recall in 2015, and Samsung’s Galaxy Note 7 recall in 2016), it is important to examine whether the earnings pressure that managers experience shapes their production decisions and in turn, the likelihood of product quality failures. Managers’ efforts to meet or beat earnings expectations by manipulating real activities are likely to engender product recalls for several reasons. First, managers may reduce investments in production facilities and cut discretionary expenses in production in pursuing short-term earnings targets (e.g.,He and Tian 2013,Irani and Oesch 2016,Caskey and Ozel 2017). Prior studies document that many product defects stem from insufficient investments and expenditures in the design, manufacture, and maintenance processes (Connally 2009,Taylor 2011,Shah et al. 2017). As such, firms may have more product defects and hence, a higher likelihood of recalls if managers curtail investments and discretionary expenses in trying to reach earnings targets. Second, in striving to meet or beat earnings expectations, managers may overproduce in order to boost earnings by reducing the cost of goods sold (e.g.,Thomas and Zhang 2002,Roychowdhury 2006). Overproduction could result in overutilization and lack of maintenance of plants, excessive employee workloads, and inadequate product quality monitoring. To the extent that managers engage in overproduction to boost short-term earnings, we expect the product quality to deteriorate, translating into more product recalls. Nevertheless, instilling tension into the analysis, it is possible that in taking steps to facilitate meeting earnings targets, firms may benefit in the form of enjoying better access to external financing and attracting more favorable financing terms. Relaxing their financial constraints enables firms to invest more in production, which in turn, lowers the incidence of product recalls (Kini et al. 2017). Further, improving their financial condition enables firms to sustain investments in the maintenance process, such as strict quality control, better packaging, and adequate warnings or instructions, which may also result in recalls becoming less likely. It is also plausible that pursuing earnings targets has no material impact on product recalls because managers may only engage in real activities management when its impact on operations is small (Graham et al. 2005). Given the severe adverse impact that product recalls can have on recalling firms and their customers, managers facing pressure to meet or beat earnings benchmarks may, for example, refrain from cutting discretionary investment in production that can lead to serious product failures. Accordingly, it amounts to an empirical question whether product recalls are sensitive to managers’ manipulation of real activities in striving to reach earnings targets. To examine this research question, we hand collect data on product recalls from four regulatory agencies in the United States: the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC), the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), and the U.S. Coast Guard (USCG). We gauge the earnings target with the consensus analyst forecast and designate a firm as suspected of meeting or beating earnings benchmarks if the difference between its actual earnings per share (EPS) and the average analyst forecasted EPS is between zero and two cents. Consistent with prior research (Cohen and Zarowin 2010,Zang 2012), we also construct an aggregate measure of a firm’s real earnings management activities. The combination of these two variables captures the firm’s suspected benchmark beating through real earnings management (hereafter SBBREM). In analyzing a sample of 11,434 firm-year observations covering the 2001–2017 period, we find that SBBREM is associated with a higher likelihood and frequency of recalls. Reflecting the first-order economic impact according to our coefficient estimates, we find that compared with non-SBBREM firms, SBBREM firms experience a 10.92% (57.80%) increase in the likelihood (number) of product recalls relative to the sample mean. This evidence lends support to the narrative that the pressure to meet or beat earnings targets motivates firms to orchestrate the manipulation of real activities that leads to more product recalls. In extensively analyzing whether our core results are robust, we continue to find supportive evidence. We also undertake several tests designed to alleviate endogeneity threats to reliable identification. The results suggest that endogeneity problems are unlikely to be spuriously responsible for the documented impact of SBBREM on product recalls. Moreover, we conduct two falsification tests. First, we find that managers’ attempts to meet or beat earnings targets through accrual-based earnings management do not alter firms’ real business operations and exert no impact on recalls. Second, after respecifying the dependent variable to the likelihood and frequency of recalls one, two, and three years ago in successive regressions, we reassuringly find that the coefficients on SBBREM are generally insignificant. This helps dispel the concern that our main results stem from correlated omitted variables that drive both recalls and SBBREM. Next, we explore cross-sectional variation in the relation between SBBREM and product recalls. First, we find that the impact of SBBREM on product recalls is magnified for firms whose managers have stronger incentives to manipulate earnings. Our evidence implies that these managers are more eager to manage earnings upward (e.g.,Graham et al. 2005,Bergstresser and Philippon 2006), which distorts firm operations and leads to more product failures. Second, we report that the importance of SBBREM to product recalls falls for firms subject to more discipline in the form of customer power. These results suggest that powerful customers constrain managerial opportunistic behaviors in the production process by imposing stricter monitoring and threatening to end cooperation (e.g.,Patatoukas 2012,Crawford et al. 2020). Third, we find that the role that SBBREM plays in product recalls is concentrated in firms with fewer growth opportunities. This evidence implies that investments in product quality can lead to higher future value for firms with more growth opportunities. Moreover, these firms experience less severe agency problems (Arikan and Stulz 2016), also reducing the impact of managerial opportunism on product recalls. Finally, we report that the incidence of recalls has a negative impact on firms’ future performance evident in their valuations and sales growth. We make several contributions to extant research. First, we extend prior work on the consequences of earnings pressure by showing that managers’ efforts to meet or beat earnings expectations through real earnings manipulation could lead to more product recalls. Most prior research on this issue focuses on the economic implications of meeting earnings targets from the perspective of shareholders and documents that firms sacrifice long-term firm value in pursuing short-term earnings objectives (Bhojraj et al. 2009,Ertan 2022). However, recent studies have begun to explore the fallout for other stakeholders of firms striving to meet the market’s earnings expectations (Caskey and Ozel 2017,Thomas et al. 2022). We add to prior work by showing that earnings pressure could distort the firm’s production process, resulting in product quality failures. Given that product quality is typically central to firms’ long-term success and that recalls are detrimental to customers, we detect a negative impact of earnings pressure for both shareholders and customers. Further, prior studies find that managers in striving to reach earnings targets undertake real activities manipulation (e.g.,Roychowdhury 2006). Our analysis complements prior work by documenting a severe outcome of real activities manipulation driven by managers pursuing short-term earnings targets. Finally, we provide evidence implying that lowering product quality in attempting to reach earnings targets is not smart because the associated product recalls engender long-term negative consequences for the firm. Accordingly, we extend prior research by quantifying how much firms are willing to sacrifice (i.e., bearing the risk of future product recalls) for engaging in real earnings management to meet or beat earnings targets. Second, we advance product recall research by analyzing the role that SBBREM plays in the incidence of recalls. Although product recalls impose large costs on both customers and recalling firms, prior work seldom examines the determinants of recalls, likely reflecting that large-scale product recall data are not easily accessible. As such, most studies focus on a small sample of firms or a single industry (e.g.,Wowak et al. 2015,Shah et al. 2017), althoughKini et al. (2017,2022) are major exceptions. In leveraging a comprehensive data set on product recalls, we complement prior evidence by showing that managers’ opportunistic real earnings management activities in striving to meet earnings targets could lead to more recalls. As such, our study appears to be the first to document the impact of SBBREM on product markets, which is related to everyday life and is central to firm operations.
article.2. Institutional Background and Hypothes: 2.1. Institutional Background Product recalls are the outcome of severe product quality failures, which can stem from design flaws, manufacturing faults, product contamination, poor packaging, inadequate warnings or instructions, lax inspection, etc. Product recalls are prevalent in the consumer market, and these events routinely have major economic and social consequences. For example, U.S. federal agencies report that at least 4,000 product recalls occurred each year from 2010 to 2015.1Product recalls have substantial adverse effects on customers and the recalling firms. In fact, recalled products are often responsible for customers suffering serious injuries or even death. For example, Merck & Co. pulled its Vioxx product from the market in September 2004 after studies revealed that the arthritis medication led to a steep rise in the risk of fatal heart attacks and strokes. FDA investigators later estimated that at the time of its recall, Vioxx had been taken by some 4 million Americans, leading to 140,000 heart attacks that were responsible for 60,000 deaths.2 Product recalls engender direct costs for the recalling firms, such as the costs of replacing or repairing the defective products. Firms may also incur major indirect costs, including the loss of goodwill, damage to their brand name reputations, the loss of sales, and even exposure to product liability lawsuits (Jarrell and Peltzman 1985,Hoffer et al. 1988,Barber and Darrough 1996). Accordingly, product recalls are costly to recalling firms, leading to their shareholders suffering a significant wealth loss (Jarrell and Peltzman 1985,Pruitt and Peterson 1986,Lee et al. 2015,Kini et al. 2017). For example, in 2008, Takata made one of the most complex and expensive recalls in history. The National Highway Safety Transportation Board expected the recall to cover more than 37 million vehicles involving 49.5 million airbag inflators in the United States alone. Takata filed for bankruptcy in 2017. The regulators had forecasted that the company could not finish the recall and fix the defective airbags until 2023.3In 2016, Samsung recalled about 2.5 million units of its new Galaxy Note 7 smartphones because of a flaw in the device’s batteries that made them catch fire and explode. After the recall, Samsung’s shares fell more than 8%, erasing $17 billion from the company’s market value.4From 2015 to 2018, the CPSC imposed civil penalties relating to recalls against companies that included at least $25 million in fines each year.5According to a survey of firms belonging to the Grocery Manufacturers Association that had experienced a recall in the preceding five years, 29% of the firms reported financial losses ranging from $10 to $29 million as a result of a product recall; 5% of them incurred losses exceeding $100 million.6 2.2. Hypothesis Development In survey evidence,Graham et al. (2005)report that corporate executives focus intently on meeting or beating earnings targets. Managers facing pressure from earnings expectations can either manipulate accruals or manage real activities to boost reported earnings (Roychowdhury 2006,Cohen et al. 2008,Dechow et al. 2010). Compared with accruals manipulation, managers’ real activities in exaggerating earnings may fly under the radar given that they are difficult for investors to detect in the short run, although this form of manipulation likely undermines long-term firm performance (e.g.,Bhojraj et al. 2009,Kothari et al. 2016). In a major downside, managing firm real activities in attempting to reach earnings targets may translate into severe product failures that necessitate recalls in some cases. At an operational level, managers may reduce investments in production facilities and cut discretionary expenses in the production process in responding to short-term earnings target incentives (e.g.,He and Tian 2013,Irani and Oesch 2016,Caskey and Ozel 2017). Extensive prior research implies that many product defects stem from inadequate investments and expenditures in the design, manufacture, and maintenance processes, which lead to obsolete production technology, inadequate maintenance of machinery, overutilization of plants, inadequate training, materials cost cutting, and the failure to implement rigorous quality control systems (Connally 2009,Taylor 2011,Shah et al. 2017). Additionally, financial distress or having excessive debt in their capital structures may force firms to reduce investments in production, precipitating more recalls (e.g.,Maksimovic and Titman 1991,Phillips and Sertsios 2013,Kini et al. 2017). In analyzing the automotive industry,Shah et al. (2017)document that insufficient investments in capacity and labor tend to lead to overutilization of plants and overreliance on overtime pay and temporary workers, which in turn, increase the incidence of product recalls. If managers cut investments and discretionary expenses in striving to reach earnings targets, we would expect their firms to suffer more production defects, resulting in a higher likelihood of product recalls. In another way to meet or beat the market’s expectations, firms may elect to overproduce in order to reduce the cost of goods sold and boost reported earnings (e.g.,Thomas and Zhang 2002,Roychowdhury 2006).7Importantly, overproduction likely causes overutilization and lack of maintenance of plants, excessive employee workloads, and lax product inspections, raising the likelihood of product defects. Accordingly, if managers overproduce in pursuing earnings targets, we would expect their firms to have poorer product quality that may culminate in more recalls.8 In short, managers may cut investments and discretionary expenses as well as engage in overproduction in striving to reach earnings targets. Our prediction reflects that this behavior leads to product failures becoming more frequent, which in turn, results in more product recalls. Firms suspected of manipulating real activities to meet or beat the market’s earnings expectations are more likely to suffer product recalls. However, steps taken in pursuing short-term earnings benchmarks may translate into a reduction in product recalls. By managing earnings to meet or beat market expectations, firms may secure more access to external financing and enjoy more favorable financing terms.Watts and Zimmerman (1990)argue that firms may manage their earnings to reduce the probability of violating a loan covenant, translating into cheaper debt financing.Burgstahler (1997)suggests that managing earnings to avoid earnings decreases and losses can increase a firm’s credit rating.Trueman and Titman (1988)provide evidence that firms exploit real activities management to smooth reported income in order to attract lower borrowing costs.Bhojraj et al. (2009)find that firms that reduce their discretionary expenditures to beat forecasts issue more equity afterward. Reinforcing their research,Graham et al. (2005, p. 27) find that 86.3% of surveyed executives “believe that meeting benchmarks builds credibility with the capital market.” Indeed, prior research explores how the financial condition of a firm affects its ability and incentives to invest in product quality. For example,Maksimovic and Titman (1991)argue that firms with more debt in their capital structures suffer investment distortions that might force them to cut discretionary investments in product quality. In analyzing the supermarket industry,Matsa (2011)finds that higher leverage is associated with greater product shortfalls.Phillips and Sertsios (2013)examine the airline industry and document that baggage handling and on-time performance fall when airlines experience financial distress.Kini et al. (2017)report that arranging external financing on attractive terms alleviates firms’ financial constraints, which enables them to invest more in production. As such, a better financial condition stemming from meeting or beating earnings targets may lead to increased investment in production, which in turn, can result in recalls becoming less likely. Further, improving their financial condition also enables firms to sustain investments in the maintenance process, which may also reduce the incidence of recalls. Additionally, it is plausible that the incidence of product recalls is insensitive to managers’ efforts to meet or beat earnings targets. Because recalls are the outcome of severe product quality failures, meeting or beating earnings targets may have no material impact on product recalls given that survey evidence implies that managers will only engage in real activities manipulation when its impact on firm operations is minimal (Graham et al. 2005). Specifically,Graham et al. (2005, p. 40) highlight that interviewed the Chief Financial Officer participants admit that they are tempted to initiate real activities management “as long as the actions are within Generally Accepted Accounting Principles and the real sacrifices are not too large.” Given the severe adverse impact that product recalls can have on recalling firms and their customers, managers facing pressure to meet or beat earnings benchmarks may, for example, refrain from cutting discretionary investment in production that could lead to serious product failures. Consequently, the implications of firms relying on real activities in striving to reach earnings targets may be too small to shape the likelihood of product recalls. It may be difficult to accept that managers eager to meet or beat the market’s earnings expectations would resort to such extensive real activities manipulation that a product recall becomes more likely. Accordingly, the role that managers’ manipulation of real activities in trying to meet or beat earnings benchmarks plays in shaping the incidence of product recalls distils to an empirical question.
article.3. Data and Sample: 3.1. Sample Selection We gather the data used in this study from multiple sources. We hand collect data on product recalls from four regulatory agencies in the United States that govern product quality and safety there. Data on food, drug, and medical device recalls are available from the weekly enforcement reports published by the FDA. Data on consumer product recalls are collected from the CPSC, which covers recalls of numerous industries, such as children’s products, household appliances, home furnishings, toys, nursery products, workshop tools, and yard equipment. Data on automobile recalls are retrieved from the NHTSA. Finally, we obtain data on recalls of boats and related products from the USCG. Each recall announcement that regulators make covers the product being recalled, the manufacturer, the recall volume, and the recall date. We download firm financial and customer data from Compustat, analyst forecast data from The Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System, stock return data from Center for Research of Security Prices, executive compensation data from ExecuComp, merger and acquisition data from Thompson Financial SDC Platinum (SDC), and institutional ownership data from Thomson Financial. We manually match firms in the product recall data with firms in Compustat based on their names. If searching by the parent name does not yield a match, we conduct additional searches to identify whether the name of the recalling firm matches any of its subsidiaries in Compustat. We restrict the sample period to 2001–2017 given that the number of recall events is limited prior to 2001.9 After beginning with all the firm-year observations in Compustat during our sample period, we exclude firms operating in financial and utility industries (Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) codes 6000–6999 and 4900–4999). We also exclude firm-years without any analyst forecasts because our earnings expectation measure is constructed based on analyst earnings forecasts. Further, we followBhojraj et al. (2009)by removing firm-years with total assets under $10 million to ensure that the presence of microcap or penny stocks does not bias our results. We also drop firm-years with missing values for any of the variables used in the baseline analysis. Finally, we followKini et al. (2017)by excluding firms that belong to a three-digit SIC industry for which no member―whether the focal firm or a competitor―has issued a product recall during the years under study.10To mitigate the effect of outliers, we winsorize all continuous variables at both the 1st and 99th percentiles. Our final sample consists of 11,434 firm-year observations for 1,896 unique firms.11 Table 1reports the distribution of recall events and firm-year observations in our sample. Panel A ofTable 1presents the sample distribution by the Fama–French 12 industries. The consumer durables industry has the largest number of recalls, whereas the business equipment industry contributes the largest number of observations. The healthcare, medical equipment, and drugs (oil, gas, and coal extraction and products) industry has the highest (lowest) fraction of observations with at least one recall. Panel B ofTable 1reports the sample distribution by year, which shows that 2013 had the largest number of recalls and that 2017 had the largest number of observations. The fraction of observations with at least one recall is the highest in 2016 and the lowest in 2001 and 2002.12 Table 1.Sample Distribution Table 1.Sample Distribution Notes. This table reports the sample distribution by industry and by year. The sample period is 2001–2017. 3.2. Variables Our dependent variables are the likelihood (INCIDENCE) and frequency (FREQ) of product recalls. Consistent with prior research (e.g.,Kini et al. 2017), we specifyINCIDENCEas a dummy variable equal to one for firms with at least one product recall during the year and zero otherwise. We defineFREQas the natural logarithm of one plus the number of recalls during the year. Our independent variable of interest isSBBREM, which reflects the interaction between suspected benchmark beating (SUSPECT) and high real earnings management (HREM).SUSPECTis a dummy variable set to one for firms with an earnings surprise between zero and two cents in the year and zero otherwise. Consistent withCaskey and Ozel (2017), we calculate the earnings surprise as the gap between a firm’s actual EPS and the average analyst forecasted EPS.13HREMis a dummy variable equal to one for firms whose real earnings management is above the industry-year median and zero otherwise. We useSBBREMrather than stand-aloneHREMbecauseSBBREMbetter captures firms’ opportunistic real earnings management activities. There are two competing views on managers’ incentives to engage in real earnings management according to prior research. The signaling view suggests that managers conduct real earnings management to signal their private information to capital market participants (e.g.,Gunny 2010,Al-Shattarat et al. 2022). Considering the costly nature of real earnings management, managers manipulate real activities when the benefit of real earnings management derived from the information conveyed to the capital markets outweighs the negative impact (Zhao et al. 2012). The managerial opportunism view holds that managers exploit real earnings management to disguise the firm’s true economic performance and misinform financial statement users (e.g.,Roychowdhury 2006). Empirically, it is difficult to distinguish the signaling and the opportunistic incentives of real earnings management. However, there is evidence that managers undertake real earnings management activities to meet or beat earnings benchmarks. For example,Cohen et al. (2010)find that managers opportunistically use monthly advertising as a real earnings management tool to reach quarterly earnings targets. Managers may also myopically rely on real earnings management to meet or beat earnings targets to secure private contractual benefits (Cohen and Zarowin 2010,Kothari et al. 2016). As such, it is possible that real earnings management activities not designed for meeting or beating earnings benchmarks are mainly for signaling purposes or are not opportunistic enough to severely disrupt firms’ product design and production process. Given that recalls are the outcome of severe product quality failures, such activities may not lead to an increase in product recalls. In this design,SBBREMfully captures the treatment effect: that is, the difference in recall likelihood and frequency between firms likely to meet or beat earnings benchmarks through income increasing real earnings management (i.e., firms withSUSPECT= 1 andHREM= 1) and the remaining firms (i.e., firms withSUSPECT= 1 andHREM= 0, firms withSUSPECT= 0 andHREM= 1, and firms withSUSPECT= 0 andHREM= 0). We follow prior research in selecting and specifying these control variables (e.g.,Kini et al. 2017): firm size (LOGASSET), leverage (LEVERAGE), cash flow shock (FCFSHOCK), fixed asset density (PPE), the market to book ratio (MB), total factor productivity (TFP), market concentration (HHI), market power (MKTSHARE), the number of major suppliers (SUPPLIER), and the credit rating (RATING).14Detailed variable definitions are available in the appendix. InTable 2, we report some summary statistics on the variables in the baseline analysis. The mean value of recall likelihood is 0.0595, indicating that 5.95% of the sample observations experience at least one product recall, and the mean value of recall frequency is 0.0897. The mean value of SBBREM is 0.0934, reflecting that 9.34% of observations are suspected of reaching earnings targets through real earnings management.15The distributional statistics for the control variables closely resemble those reported in prior work (e.g.,Kini et al. 2017,2022). Table 2.Summary Statistics Table 2.Summary Statistics Notes. This table reports the summary statistics of the variables in the baseline analysis. The sample period is 2001–2017. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix.
article.4. SBBREM and Product Recalls: 4.1. Baseline Analysis In this section, we examine in a multivariate framework whether managers in attempting to meet or beat earnings benchmarks through real earnings management affect the likelihood and frequency of product recalls. The regression specification is as follows: InTable 3, we report the results of the baseline analysis. Columns (1) and (3) inTable 3present the results with industry and year fixed effects but without control variables, whereas columns (2) and (4) inTable 3present the results of our full baseline model. The dependent variable in columns (1) and (2) inTable 3isINCIDENCE. In both columns, the coefficient onSBBREMis positive and statistically significant, implying that compared with non-SBBREM firms, SBBREM firms exhibit a higher likelihood of product recalls. Specifically, the marginal effect ofSBBREMin column (2) inTable 3is 0.0065, indicating that the likelihood of a recall is 0.65 percentage points higher for SBBREM firms than non-SBBREM firms. Given that the mean likelihood of a product recall in our sample is 5.95 percentage points, this constitutes a 10.92% increase relative to the mean. The dependent variable in columns (3) and (4) inTable 3isFREQ. Again, the coefficient onSBBREMenters positively in both columns, suggesting that SBBREM firms experience a higher recall frequency than non-SBBREM firms. The coefficient in column (4) inTable 3suggests that the recall frequency is 0.0484 higher for SBBREM firms than non-SBBREM firms. Because the sample mean of the recall frequency is 0.0897, the coefficient implies that SBBREM increases the number of recalls by 57.80%.20In short, the impact is both statistically and economically significant.21Collectively, the baseline evidence supports that firms suspected of manipulating real activities in pursuing earnings targets have a higher likelihood and frequency of product recalls, consistent with our hypothesis. Table 3.Suspected Benchmark Beating Through Real Earnings Management and Product Recalls Table 3.Suspected Benchmark Beating Through Real Earnings Management and Product Recalls Notes. This table reports the regression results of the effect of suspected benchmark beating through real earnings management on product recalls. The sample period is 2001–2017. The regressions are performed by probit (OLS) when the dependent variable isINCIDENCE(FREQ), withz-statistics (t-statistics) corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustered at the firm level. We report the marginal effect of the coefficients for probit regressions. Constant, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. FE, fixed effect. *Significance at the 10% level; **significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level. 4.2. Robustness Tests To evaluate whether our baseline results are robust, we conduct various sensitivity tests and report the results inTable 4. First, we examine whether our findings remain when we isolate the two different forms of real earnings manipulation: overproducing and cutting discretionary expenses.22SBBREM_ABPRODis the interaction term ofSUSPECTandHREM_ABPROD, whereHREM_ABPRODis a dummy variable equal to one for firms with abnormal production costs above the industry-year median and zero otherwise.SBBREM_ABDISEXis the interaction term ofSUSPECTandHREM_ABDISEX, whereHREM_ABDISEXis a dummy variable equal to one for firms with abnormal discretionary expenses below the industry-year median and zero otherwise. We re-estimate the model after replacingSBBREMwithSBBREM_ABPRODandSBBREM_ABDISEXin successive regressions. In the results reported in panel A ofTable 4, we find that the coefficients onSBBREM_ABPRODandSBBREM_ABDISEXare both positive and significant, suggesting that meeting or beating earnings targets through both forms of real earnings manipulation triggers product recalls. Table 4.Robustness Tests Table 4.Robustness Tests Notes. This table reports the results of the robustness tests on the effect of suspected benchmark beating through real earnings management on product recalls. The sample period is 2001–2017. The regressions are performed by probit (OLS) when the dependent variable isINCIDENCE(FREQ), withz-statistics (t-statistics) corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustered at the firm level. We report the marginal effect of the coefficients for probit and negative binomial regressions. Constant, control variables, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes, and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. FE, fixed effect. **Significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level. Second, we analyze whether our results hold when we narrow or widen the threshold by one cent in defining suspected benchmark beating.SBBREM_1Cis the interaction term ofSUSPECT_1CandHREM, whereSUSPECT_1Ccaptures earnings surprises between zero and one cent. Similarly,SBBREM_3Cis the interaction term ofSUSPECT_3CandHREM, whereSUSPECT_3Ccaptures earnings surprises between zero and three cents. After replacingSBBREMwithSBBREM_1CandSBBREM_3Cin successive regressions, we report in panel B ofTable 4that the coefficients on both variables are positive and significant, implying that we continue to find supportive evidence under alternative thresholds in defining suspected benchmark beating. Third, we consider whether our core evidence persists under alternative measures of analyst earnings expectations.Bhojraj et al. (2009)use analyst consensus forecasts as of the second month before the end of the year, allowing managers more time to exploit real activities in managing earnings.Roychowdhury (2006)relies on the average of each analyst’s latest forecast issued prior to the earnings announcement date to gauge analyst expectations. After specifyingSUSPECT_RandSUSPECT_BHPMconsistent withRoychowdhury (2006)andBhojraj et al. (2009), respectively, we generateSBBREM_RandSBBREM_BHPMby separately interacting these two measures withHREM. We re-estimateEquation (1)usingSBBREM_BHPMandSBBREM_R. In the results reported in panel C ofTable 4, we find that the coefficients on both variables are positive and significant, reflecting that our earlier evidence holds using other intervals in calculating analyst expectations. Fourth, in an alternative fixed effects structure, we re-estimate the baseline model inEquation (1)after replacing industry and year fixed effects with industry-by-year fixed effects. Prior work documents that products in the maturity stage usually have higher quality compared with those in the introduction or growth stages and that technological advances in the industry routinely lead to improvements in product quality and safety (e.g.,Hall 1980,MacMillan et al. 1982,Anderson and Zeithaml 1984). In panel D ofTable 4, we verify that our main results continue to hold after including industry-by-year fixed effects to account for unobserved, time-varying industry-level factors, such as product life cycles and technological changes. Fifth, we explore whether our results are driven by the financial crisis. It is plausible that market-wide economic shocks, such as the financial crisis, affect both managers’ incentive to exaggerate earnings and the likelihood of product failures, engendering an association between SBBREM and product recalls. Although including year fixed effects in the baseline regression alleviates this concern to a certain extent, we run a robustness check by excluding the financial crisis period (i.e., 2007–2008) to further confront this issue. We re-estimateEquation (1)using the restricted sample and report the results in panel E ofTable 4. The coefficient onSBBREMremains positive and significant in both regressions, implying that our core results are materially insensitive to no longer including the financial crisis period in the analysis. Sixth, we analyze whether our evidence is robust to using an alternative regression method. This involves usingCOUNTS, which reflects the number of recalls a firm experiences during a year, as the dependent variable. We followWowak et al. (2015)by specifying a negative binomial distribution with a log link function for this test. After re-estimatingEquation (1)using negative binomial regression, we report the results in panel F ofTable 4, which include that the coefficient onSBBREMremains positive and highly significant, reinforcing our earlier evidence. Seventh, in the baseline analysis, we use one-year laggedSBBREMacross all products. However, it is possible that some defective products are recalled beyond one year after the manufacturing date, admitting measurement error into the analysis. Accordingly, we conduct two tests to address this concern. First, we specify two- and three-year laggedSBBREMand report the results in panel G ofTable 4. Our main results hold in all regressions. Second, we separately analyze the impact of SBBREM on recalls of durable and nondurable goods. Durable goods are commodities that can be stored or inventoried and have an average service life exceeding three years (e.g., motor vehicles), whereas nondurable goods are commodities that can be stored or inventoried and have an average `service life of at most three years (e.g., foods and drugs) (Gomes et al. 2009). We divide our sample into durable and nondurable goods subsamples based on the SIC classification of these two types. Afterward, we re-estimate the baseline regression inEquation (1)for both subsamples. In the results reported in panel H ofTable 4, we find that the coefficient onSBBREMis significantly positive for both durable and nondurable goods, suggesting that one-year laggedSBBREMshapes product recalls for both types of goods. This evidence implies that using one-year laggedSBBREMacross different products is defensible. Finally, we examine whether our results are sensitive to controlling for several variables that reflect firms’ access to external financing, including financing slack (SLACK), interest coverage (COVER), the Kaplan and Zingales (KZ) index (KZINDEX), and firm age (AGE). In panel I ofTable 4, we report that the coefficient onSBBREMcontinues to enter positively, consistent with our earlier evidence.23 4.3. Falsification Tests Additionally, we conduct two falsification tests. First, rather than distorting their business operations, firms eager to reach earnings targets may resort to manipulating accounting accruals, although this should be irrelevant to the incidence of product recalls. As such, we expect the impact of suspected benchmark beating on product recalls to be insensitive to accrual-based earnings management, which we measure with discretionary accruals (AEM_DSS) calculated consistent withDechow et al. (1995), the Dechow and Dichev (DD) measure ofDechow and Dichev (2002)adjusted consistent withMcNichols (2002)(AEM_DD), and auditor choice (BIG4).24Next, we specifyHAEM_DSS(HAEM_DD) as a dummy variable set to one if a firm’sAEM_DSS(AEM_DD) exceeds the industry-year median and zero otherwise.BIG4is a dummy variable set to one for firms that appoint Big Four auditors and zero otherwise. We separately interactHAEM_DSS,HAEM_DD, andBIG4withSUSPECTso that the interaction termsSBBAEM_DSS,SBBAEM_DD, andSBBAEM_BIG4capture the impact of both meeting or beating earnings targets and accrual-based earnings manipulation. Afterward, we regress the incidence and frequency of recalls against the new interaction term and the same set of control variables as in our baseline regression inEquation (1). The results in panel A ofTable 5include that the coefficients onSBBAEM_DSS,SBBAEM_DD, andSBBAEM_BIG4are generally statistically insignificant, implying that firms’ product recalls are insensitive to meeting or beating earnings targets through accrual-based earnings manipulation, which does not affect firms’ real business operations.25 Table 5.Falsification Tests Table 5.Falsification Tests Notes. This table reports the regression results of falsification tests. The sample period is 2001–2017. The regressions are performed by probit (OLS) when the dependent variable isINCIDENCE(FREQ), withz-statistics (t-statistics) corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustered at the firm level. We report the marginal effect of the coefficients for probit regressions. Constant, control variables, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes, and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. FE, fixed effect. *Significance at the 10% level; **significance at the 5% level. Second, in successive estimations, we regress the likelihood and frequency of recalls one year ago, two years ago, and three years ago against SBBREM in the current year and all the controls inEquation (1). In the results reported in panel B ofTable 5, we find thatSBBREMfails to load in five of the six regressions. Reassuringly, this evidence collectively helps dispel the concern that our main finding stems from correlated omitted variables that drive both product recalls and meeting or beating earnings targets through real earnings management activities.
article.5. Cross-Sectional Tests: 5.1. Managerial Incentives Although SBBREM is intended to capture managers’ opportunistic real earnings management, like many other measures in empirical studies, it is an imperfect measure. For example, it remains possible that managers strategically use real earnings management to meet or beat earnings targets in striving to signal future performance to capital market participants. Further, the damage that real earnings management inflicts on the firm’s production process stems from not only the level of real earnings management but also how real earnings management is conducted. Managers who are more eager to meet or beat earnings benchmarks may adopt more aggressive real earnings management methods. For example, these managers may cut expenses nearly across the board, irrespective of whether these expenses are central to the production process (e.g., Research and Development Expenses (R&D) and quality control). In contrast, managers with weaker incentives may manage real earnings in a milder way. For example, they may cut expenses more selectively, including by only reducing those that are less detrimental to the firm’s production (e.g., office supplies and business entertainment). Additionally, they may manage real earnings more strategically by planning the cut in advance to minimize the disruption to firm operations. To deepen the analysis of the opportunistic nature of real earnings management, we examine the role that managerial incentives play in shaping the relation between SBBREM and product recalls. We expect that when managerial incentives to reach earnings targets are stronger, SBBREM is better able to capture opportunistic real earnings management, which in turn, leads to more product recalls. Prior research documents that career concerns motivate managers to orchestrate upward earnings management (e.g.,Graham et al. 2005,Bergstresser and Philippon 2006). There is also evidence that media reporting on firms’ short-term performance could induce managerial short-termism, which impedes long-term corporate investment (Dai et al. 2021). If managers cut quality-related expenditures and overproduce in pursuing earnings targets, then the impact of SBBREM on product recalls should be amplified in firms whose managers have greater career concerns and firms subject to more extensive media coverage. Consistent with prior research (e.g.,Armstrong et al. 2012), we gauge executives’ career concerns with the firm’s ex ante takeover probability (TAKEOVER), estimated as the predicted value from the logit regression specified inBillett and Xue (2007). Executives are routinely terminated after their firms are acquired. To deter potential acquirers, managers in firms facing a greater takeover threat may manage earnings upward to reach earnings targets in order to keep their stock prices inflated. We define media coverage (MEDIA) as the number of earnings-related news reports issued on the firm during the year. Given prior research on the disciplinary role the media plays (e.g.,Dai et al. 2021), we expect that managers who face more intensive earnings-related media coverage are more likely to cut quality-related expenditures and overproduce in pursuing earnings targets. We separately bisect the sample according to the median value of one-year lagged takeover probability and media coverage. After re-estimatingEquation (1)for each subsample, we report the results inTable 6. In panel A ofTable 6, the coefficient onSBBREMenters positively when we isolate firms subject to a high takeover probability, whereas it is insignificant for the subsample with low takeover probability. The differences between the coefficients are statistically significant as well. In panel B ofTable 6, the coefficient onSBBREMis positive and significant for the subsample with high media coverage, whereas it fails to load for the subsample with low media coverage. In both cases, there is a perceptible difference between the coefficients. Consistent with expectations, the evidence collectively implies that the importance of SBBREM to recalls intensifies when executives have stronger incentives to manage earnings.26 Table 6.The Effect of Managerial Incentives Table 6.The Effect of Managerial Incentives Notes. This table reports the regression results of the effect of managerial incentives on the relation between suspected benchmark beating through real earnings management and product recalls. The sample period is 2001–2017. The regressions are performed by probit (OLS) when the dependent variable isINCIDENCE(FREQ), with the marginal effects (coefficients) reported out of parentheses andz-statistics (t-statistics) reported in parentheses. Thez-statistics (t-statistics) are corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustered at the firm level. Constant, control variables, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes, and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. Empiricalp-values are calculated from a bootstrapping procedure with 500 replications to estimate the significance of observed differences in coefficients ofSBBREMbetween two subsamples. FE, fixed effect. **Significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level. 5.2. Customer Power Next, we explore whether the relation between SBBREM and product recalls varies with customer power. We expect to observe that the positive impact of SBBREM on product recalls subsides for firms whose customers are more powerful or important to firms given that these customers can constrain the managerial opportunism that may lead to product failures by actively monitoring and threatening to end cooperation. We consider two groups of important customers. The first group is large corporate customers that are valuable to the firm because losing them could have severe adverse effects on its financial condition and operations (e.g.,Johnson et al. 2010,Patatoukas 2012).27In the event that a supplier experiences a product recall, its large corporate customers usually reduce demand for its products and even start to develop products internally or vertically integrate the supplier (e.g.,Reilly and Hoffer 1983,Porter 1985). Also, large corporate customers usually have private information about the firm (Crawford et al. 2020), which enables them to better monitor the supplier’s manufacturing process. It follows that the presence of a large corporate customer constrains managerial opportunistic behavior and disciplines the firm against trying to reach earnings targets by manipulating real activities that can, in turn, undermine product quality. Consistent withDhaliwal et al. (2016), we measure the power of corporate customers with corporate customer concentration (CHHI), calculated as the sum of the squares of the ratio of a firm’s sales to each corporate customer during the year to the firm’s total sales during the year. We identify corporate customers from Compustat’s Customer Segment files. The higher the corporate customer concentration, the greater the power that these customers possess. The second group is composed of government customers.28Firms value government customers because these customers not only are less likely to default or go bankrupt but also, they tend to sign large-scale and long-term procurement contracts with firms (Goldman et al. 2013). Prior research documents that government customers closely monitor their suppliers to verify that they use adequate financial resources in fulfilling the contracts and that their accounting systems accurately track direct and indirect costs of goods (e.g.,Feldman and Keyes 2011,Samuels 2021). Consequently, the presence of government customers implies stricter oversight of the firm’s production process, which may deter managers from engaging in earnings manipulation activities that could lead to product recalls. We define government customer (CGOV) as a dummy variable equal to one if the firm has at least one government customer according to Compustat’s Customer Segment files and zero otherwise. In successive analyses, we partition the sample according to the median value of one-year laggedCHHIand one-year laggedCGOV. We re-estimateEquation (1)for each subsample and report the results inTable 7. In panel A ofTable 7, the coefficient onSBBREMis positive and significant for the subsample with low corporate customer concentration, whereas it is insignificant for the subsample with high corporate customer concentration. The difference between the coefficients is statistically significant for the recall frequency regression. In panel B ofTable 7, the coefficient onSBBREMis positive and significant when we narrow our focus to firms without any government customers, whereas it is insignificant for the subsample with at least one government customer.29The differences between the coefficients are significant in both cases. The results lend support to our conjecture that the presence of powerful customers constrains managers’ manipulation activities in pursuing earnings targets that can come at the expense of product quality. Table 7.The Effect of Customer Power Table 7.The Effect of Customer Power Notes. This table reports the regression results of the effect of customer power on the relation between suspected benchmark beating through real earnings management and product recalls. The sample period is 2001–2017. In panel A, the regressions are performed by probit (OLS) when the dependent variable isINCIDENCE(FREQ). In panel B, both the regressions are performed by OLS. Thez-statistics (t-statistics) are corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustered at the firm level. We report the marginal effect of the coefficients for probit regressions. Constant, control variables, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes, and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. Empiricalp-svalues are calculated from a bootstrapping procedure with 500 replications to estimate the significance of observed differences in coefficients ofSBBREMbetween two subsamples. FE, fixed effect. *Significance at the 10% level; **significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level. 5.3. Firm Growth Opportunities Next, we evaluate whether the relation between SBBREM and product recalls hinges on firm growth. Prior research suggests that managers’ myopic behavior is unlikely to dominate when incremental long-term investments can generate sufficiently high future value to outweigh the short-term benefits stemming from myopic behavior (Banker et al. 2011). Long-term investments may lead to higher future value for firms with more growth opportunities, making managers’ myopic behavior less likely to dominate in these firms. Further, agency problems are less severe in growth firms (Arikan and Stulz 2016), implying that managerial opportunism plays a smaller role in these firms. Accordingly, we expect that the positive link between SBBREM and product recalls will dissipate in firms enjoying more growth opportunities. In examining this issue, we capture a firm’s growth opportunities with its life cycle and industry sales growth in successive regressions. Consistent withBanker et al. (2011), we calculate a firm’s life cycle (LIFE_CYCLE) as the standard deviation of its sales revenue over the five years prior to the recall year divided by the mean sales revenue over the same period. We specify industry sales growth (INDSGR) as the growth rate of total sales for the firm’s three-digit SIC industry during the year. For each variable, a higher value reflects more growth opportunities. Next, we separately split the sample according to the median value of one-year lagged firm life cycle by industry and industry sales growth. We re-estimateEquation (1)for each subsample and report the results inTable 8. In panel A ofTable 8, the coefficient onSBBREMenters positively for the subsample of mature firms (i.e., whenLIFE_CYCLEis low), whereas it is insignificant for the subsample of growth firms (i.e., whenLIFE_CYCLEis high). The differences between the coefficients are statistically significant for both regressions. In panel B ofTable 8, the coefficient onSBBREMis positive and significant in all regressions. For both recall likelihood and frequency, the coefficient is larger for the subsample with low industry sales growth than that for the subsample with high industry sales growth. However, the difference in the coefficients is only significant for the recall frequency regression. Overall, the results are generally consistent with our conjecture that firms with more growth opportunities can extract higher future value from investments in product quality and are subject to less severe agency problems, translating into a weaker effect of SBBREM on recalls. article.6. Additional Analysis: Finally, we explore whether firms’ future performance suffers after a product recall. This involves specifyingTOBINSQas the firm’s Tobin’sQ, which is the ratio of the market value of assets to their book value, in yeart+ 3. The market value of assets is the sum of the market value of common stock, the book value of preferred stock, the book value of long-term debt, and the book value of current liabilities minus the difference between the book value of current assets and the book value of inventories. We codeSALEGROWas the firm’s sales in yeart+ 3 minus that in yeartdivided by sales in yeart. In separate estimations, we regress the two firm performance measures onINCIDENCEand a series of control variables. The selection of the control variables follows prior research examining the determinants of Tobin’sQand sales growth (e.g.,McConnell and Servaes 1990,Chung and Jo 1996,Daines 2001).30Because firms located in different states face different laws and market environments, we include state fixed effects in both regressions (Daines 2001,Gompers et al. 2010). In the results reported inTable 9, we find that the coefficients onINCIDENCEare negative and highly significant in both columns. This evidence implies that future performance significantly falls for firms that have a recall in the current year.31Combining this evidence with the results in our baseline analysis, we can conclude that the increased product recall risk associated with SBBREM could undermine future performance, reflecting that firms can pay a heavy price in striving to reach earnings targets through real earnings management. Table 9.Product Recalls and Firm Future Performance Table 9.Product Recalls and Firm Future Performance Notes. This table reports the regression results of the effect of product recalls on firm future performance. The sample period is 2001–2017. The regressions are performed by OLS, witht-statistics in parentheses corrected for heteroskedasticity and clustering at the firm level. Constant, control variables, industry fixed effects based on three-digit SIC codes, state fixed effects, and year fixed effects are included. The variable definitions are provided in the appendix. FE, fixed effect. *Significance at the 10% level; **significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level.
article.7. Conclusion: We examine whether managers in pursuing earnings targets through real earnings management increase the incidence of product recalls. On one hand, managers may reduce investments in production facilities and cut discretionary expenses in production in striving to meet or beat short-term earnings benchmarks. Insufficient investments and expenditures in the production process could lead to product defects, resulting in more recalls. Also, managers may overproduce to boost reported earnings by lowering the cost of goods sold. Because overproduction leads to overutilization and lack of maintenance of plants, excessive workloads for employees, and lax product quality monitoring, it likely translates into more product recalls. On the other hand, firms that reach earnings targets may enjoy better access to external financing and attract more favorable financing terms. By relaxing its financial constraints, the firm has more resources to spend on production, potentially leading to recalls becoming less likely. It is also plausible that pursuing earnings targets has no material impact on product recalls given that managers may only engage in real activities management when its impact on firm operations is minimal. Accordingly, the role that managers’ manipulation of real activities in attempting to meet or beat earnings benchmarks plays in shaping the likelihood of recalls amounts to an empirical question. In analyzing a sample of 11,434 firm-year observations spanning the 2001–2017 period, we find that SBBREM engenders a higher likelihood and frequency of product recalls. In a set of cross-sectional results consistent with expectations, we find that the importance of SBBREM to product recalls rises when executives have stronger incentives to manage earnings upward and that it falls for firms whose customers have greater power and for firms with more growth opportunities. Other evidence implies that lowering product quality to meet or beat earnings targets leads to long-term negative consequences for the firm in the form of lower Tobin’sQand sales growth. We contribute to extant research by examining the impact of managers’ activities in pursuing earnings targets through real earnings management on product recalls. Reflecting the lack of large-scale product recall data, most existing studies focus on a small sample of firms or a single industry. In contrast, we rely on comprehensive data on recalls to complement prior work by showing that managers’ benchmark beating behaviors through real earnings management are a major determinant of product recalls. We also extend research on the consequences of earnings pressure by providing evidence that managers’ opportunistic activities in striving to meet or beat market earnings expectations distort the firm’s operations to the detriment of its product quality evident in recalls. Given that product defects can impose large costs on customers and recalling firms, our research is relevant to managers, investors, customers, regulators, and broader society by alerting them to another potential downside stemming from earnings pressure. However, an important limitation of the study stems from pooling different types of recalls together in one sample, regardless of the heterogeneity in how different agencies handle recalls and the timeline of the recalls. We also urge readers to interpret our results with caution given the potential measurement error arising from our research design. Finally, although we examine the impact of product recalls on Tobin’sQand sales growth, future research could deepen the analysis of this issue by exploring other economic outcomes for firms and their customers.
